primary_diagnosis.name: Soft-tissue contusion / intramuscular haematoma, right thigh

pathophysiology_summary: A soft-tissue contusion is blunt-force disruption of muscle/subcutaneous capillaries producing focal swelling, an intramuscular haematoma and ecchymosis with pain on muscle contraction/stretch; the overlying skin is intact and the injury is benign and self-limiting over 2–4 weeks. The ground-truth task is to exclude the serious mimics/complications of significant blunt thigh trauma: femoral fracture (deformity, inability to bear weight, bony tenderness/step-off), acute compartment syndrome (pain out of proportion, tense woody compartment, paraesthesia, pain on passive stretch, a cold/pale/pulseless limb late), quadriceps rupture (palpable gap, loss of active knee extension), and later complications (DVT, myositis ossificans). Here preserved weight-bearing, a soft compressible compartment and normal neurovascular status point to an uncomplicated contusion; management is RICE, analgesia and graded mobilisation, avoiding early heat/massage.

differential.evidence_basis: Focal swelling/bruise over a blunt-impact site with intact skin and preserved weight-bearing.
Screen for deformity, inability to bear weight, bony step-off.
Screen pain-out-of-proportion, tense compartment, paraesthesia, perfusion.
Palpable gap / loss of active knee extension would suggest rupture; not present.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Contusion leads; femoral fracture and compartment syndrome must be actively excluded before conservative management.

differential.evidence_basis: Weight-bearing preserved, no deformity, soft compressible compartment, normal neurovascular status — uncomplicated contusion.
Weight-bearing and movement preserved, no deformity; escalation target if they change.
Soft compartment, proportionate pain, normal perfusion/neuro; escalation target if features develop.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After a negative fracture/compartment screen, an uncomplicated contusion leads; RICE + analgesia + graded mobilisation, with fracture/compartment/DVT safety-nets.

differential.evidence_basis: Working diagnosis; heals 2–4 weeks; watch for compartment syndrome early and DVT/myositis ossificans later.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Final position is an uncomplicated thigh contusion.

red_flags.description: Femoral fracture — deformity, inability to bear weight, bony step-off/point tenderness, severe rest pain
Acute compartment syndrome / vascular injury — pain out of proportion, tense woody swelling, paraesthesia, pain on passive stretch, cold/pale/pulseless foot

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Not screening pain-out-of-proportion/tense compartment/paraesthesia after significant blunt thigh trauma.
Heat or deep massage in the first 72h can worsen bleeding into the muscle.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Palpation for bony step-off/compartment tension and distal pulses
Assessment of active knee extension for quadriceps rupture
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: RICE + analgesia + graded mobilisation, no early heat/massage; ED for fracture/compartment features.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Missing a femoral fracture or evolving compartment syndrome.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Contusion: relative rest, ice, compression, elevation for 48–72h; simple analgesia; avoid early heat/massage; graded return to activity. Exclude fracture and compartment syndrome; watch for DVT and (late) myositis ossificans.
